The LiteLLM Thinking-Block Bug. When a model thinks before answering, its thinking content needs to be packaged correctly. LiteLLM packages it wrong, and Claude Code refuses to open the package.

The pipeline. Your prompt travels through several systems before reaching the model: Claude Code in the VM, then the Runtime local proxy, then the Tenant gateway, then llm-bridge our process, then LiteLLM where the bug is, then OpenRouter or others, then the Model, for example Opus 4.6. The model talks back along the same chain in reverse, streaming pieces of the response one at a time.

How a streaming response is shaped. The model doesn't return one big answer; it streams its response in small pieces called deltas. Each delta is wrapped inside a content block — think of a content block as a labeled envelope, and the deltas as letters inside it. There are two kinds of letters that matter here: thinking_delta, the model's reasoning, for example "step 1: 7 times 10 is 70"; and text_delta, the actual answer the user sees, for example "The answer is 91." Anthropic's rule for these streams is simple: the envelope label must match the letters inside.

A correct stream looks like this. Envelope label: thinking. thinking_delta: "step 1: 7 times 10 equals 70". thinking_delta: "step 2: 7 times 3 equals 21". thinking_delta: "step 3: 70 plus 21 equals 91". Matching letters, matching label. Then envelope label: text. text_delta: "The answer is ". text_delta: "91." Matching letters, matching label. Two envelopes, one after the other. Thinking first, then text. Each envelope's contents match its label.

What LiteLLM actually sends. When the upstream provider isn't Anthropic directly — OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, OpenAI o-series, and others — LiteLLM has to translate the response into Anthropic's envelope format. That translation has a bug: it puts every delta into one envelope, labels it text, and stuffs the thinking letters in there anyway. Envelope label: text, which is wrong. Inside: thinking_delta "step 1: 7 times 10 equals 70", thinking_delta "step 2: 7 times 3 equals 21", thinking_delta "step 3: 70 plus 21 equals 91", text_delta "The answer is 91." The label says "text" but most of the letters are "thinking" — a mismatch. Single envelope, wrong label, mixed contents. Claude Code reads this and aborts with "API returned an empty or malformed response."

What the wire actually looks like. If you're curious what the difference looks like in raw bytes, here are simplified excerpts from the actual streams we captured. The correct stream, direct from Anthropic, looks like this: event: content_block_start, data: {"content_block":{"type":"thinking"}}; event: content_block_delta, data: {"delta":{"type":"thinking_delta"}}; event: content_block_stop; event: content_block_start, data: {"content_block":{"type":"text"}}; event: content_block_delta, data: {"delta":{"type":"text_delta"}}; event: content_block_stop. The malformed stream, via LiteLLM and OpenRouter, looks like this: event: content_block_start, data: {"content_block":{"type":"text"}}; event: content_block_delta, data: {"delta":{"type":"thinking_delta"}}; event: content_block_delta, data: {"delta":{"type":"thinking_delta"}}; event: content_block_delta, data: {"delta":{"type":"text_delta"}}; event: content_block_stop.

Why LiteLLM does this. Inside LiteLLM there's a function whose job is to pick the envelope label. It looks at the first delta and runs through this checklist. One: is it a tool call? If so, label tool_use. Two: has plain text content? If so, label text. Three: has a field called thinking_blocks? If so, label thinking. Four: none of the above? Then default to text. The trap: only Anthropic's own API uses the field name thinking_blocks. Everyone else — OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Groq, OpenAI o-series — sends a field called reasoning_content or reasoning_details instead. None of those names match check number three. The function falls through to the default and labels the envelope text. Then, when the actual thinking deltas start arriving, they go into the wrongly-labeled envelope. It's a ten-line oversight: someone added detection for Anthropic's field name but forgot to add the OpenAI-compatible field names. People have reported it five separate times in the LiteLLM issue tracker; nobody has shipped a fix yet.

Our fix. We add a small piece of code on our end that watches the stream coming out of LiteLLM and corrects it before passing it on to Claude Code. Step one: hold the envelope — don't seal it yet, wait. Step two: peek at the first delta and decide the real label. Step three: stream through with the label set, deltas flowing. Step four: spot the flip — if a first text_delta arrives inside a thinking envelope, close it and open a fresh text one. A small state machine that watches the stream. For correctly-shaped responses, direct Anthropic or Bedrock, it does nothing — it just passes bytes through.

In plain English: hold the envelope. When a content_block_start event arrives, don't forward it yet — we don't know what's coming inside. Peek at the first delta. If it's a thinking_delta, set the label to thinking. Otherwise leave it as whatever LiteLLM said. Stream the rest through with the corrected label. Spot the flip. If we're in a thinking envelope and a text_delta arrives, close the thinking envelope and open a fresh text envelope — that's a proper boundary. The rewriter is "detect at runtime": it only acts when it sees the malformation. For direct Anthropic streams, which are already correctly shaped, the rewriter never triggers — bytes pass through unchanged.

The bigger picture. This bug isn't OpenRouter-specific. We confirmed it appears for Anthropic via OpenRouter — openrouter/anthropic/claude-opus-4.6; DeepSeek R1 via OpenRouter — openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1; and Gemini 3.1 Pro via OpenRouter — openrouter/google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview. And per LiteLLM's own bug tracker it also affects Groq Qwen3, VLLM, and OpenAI o-series. Five open issues. No fix shipped. We patch on our end so we don't have to wait. The day LiteLLM fixes it upstream, our rewriter quietly becomes a no-op (it only runs when it sees the malformation) and we can delete the code.

Open issues you can follow. Issue 8631 — Reasoning with OpenRouter not available while streaming. Issue 9100 — No reasoning content when streaming OpenRouter models. Issue 19849 — Groq Qwen3 reasoning missing in streaming responses. Issue 20246 — Streaming reasoning content missing for VLLM providers. Issue 20433 — anthropic.messages.acreate() with OpenAI target doesn't return reasoning content. Issue 27442 — Streaming SSE output differs from upstream for /v1/messages and it's quite slow.
